summary: Cathy O'Neil follows up on her analysis of insurance gaps in the sharing economy, specifically the dangerous period when Uber drivers have their app on but no passengers. She notes that Allstate has created a new policy to fill this gap, but questions whether the stated $15-20 annual cost is realistic. She suspects the true cost is much higher, Uber doesn't want to pay it, and if drivers must cover it themselves, their already low wages will drop further. The post illustrates how businesses built on regulatory arbitrage eventually face margin compression when rules catch up.
key_points: There's a dangerous insurance gap when Uber drivers have their app on but no passenger - a gap that has already resulted in children's deaths
Uber drivers on chat boards discuss hiding that their app was on during accidents, suggesting this gap affects everyone's insurance rates
Allstate's new $15-20/year policy to fill this gap seems suspiciously cheap, especially given Uber's fierce opposition to regulations requiring such coverage
When businesses profit by bypassing regulations, their margins fall once those regulations catch up
money_quotes: A child got killed by an Uber driver in exactly this situation. Actually more than one child.
There are chat boards of Uber drivers suggesting how to hide the fact that their app was on in case of an accident.
This supports my original theory that all of our car insurance policies will be going up because of Uber drivers.
That's kind of what happens when you create a business models that make money in part by bypassing regulations, and then the regulations catch up with you: your profit margins fall.
Uber was working very very hard to avoid this insurance from existing.
key_insight: When tech companies build profitable business models by exploiting regulatory gaps, the eventual cost of compliance reveals just how much of their margin depended on shifting risk onto workers and the public.
